title: AI That Heals: Medicine Explained

modules.title: How AI Reads Medical Images
modules.sub: Decoding what algorithms see in scans and X-rays
modules.description: Learners examine how convolutional neural networks analyze radiology images, pathology slides, and retinal scans to detect disease. They compare AI performance against human radiologists and identify scenarios where each holds an advantage. By the end, learners can articulate how image-reading AI enters clinical workflows and where human oversight remains essential.

modules.title: Predicting Disease Before It Strikes
modules.sub: Using data patterns to forecast patient health risks
modules.description: Learners explore how predictive models draw on electronic health records, genetic data, and lifestyle signals to flag patients at risk before symptoms appear. They evaluate real-world use cases such as sepsis alerts and diabetic risk scoring, weighing the clinical value against false-positive burdens. Learners leave able to critically assess a predictive tool's evidence base and practical limitations.

modules.title: AI in Drug Discovery and Research
modules.sub: Accelerating therapeutics from molecule to clinical trial
modules.description: Learners trace the drug development pipeline and pinpoint where AI shortens timelines—from protein structure prediction to candidate screening and trial design. They analyze case studies including AI-discovered antibiotics and pandemic-era vaccine research to ground the technology in real outcomes. Learners finish with a framework for evaluating claims about AI-accelerated drug development with appropriate skepticism.

modules.title: Robot-Assisted Surgery and Care
modules.sub: Understanding machines that assist, augment, and automate care
modules.description: Learners investigate how robotic systems like Da Vinci support surgeons, and how autonomous care robots assist patients in rehabilitation and elder care settings. They weigh the evidence on patient outcomes, surgeon learning curves, and liability questions that arise when a robot is in the loop. The module concludes with a structured comparison of tasks best left to humans versus those where robotics adds clear value.

modules.title: Patient Data: Benefits and Risks
modules.sub: Balancing health data utility against privacy and consent
modules.description: Learners map the journey of patient data from point of care into AI training pipelines, identifying who accesses it and under what consent frameworks. They scrutinize real breaches and regulatory responses under HIPAA, GDPR, and emerging health-AI guidelines to understand where protections succeed and fail. Learners practice evaluating a data-sharing policy from both a clinical benefit and patient rights perspective.

modules.title: Equity and Access in Health AI
modules.sub: Confronting bias and disparity embedded in medical algorithms
modules.description: Learners investigate documented cases where health AI systems performed worse for women, people of color, and low-income populations due to biased training data or flawed proxies. They apply bias-auditing concepts to assess how a hypothetical algorithm might disadvantage specific groups before deployment. The module challenges learners to propose concrete design and governance changes that move health AI toward more equitable outcomes.

modules.title: When AI Diagnosis Gets It Wrong
modules.sub: Examining failure modes, errors, and accountability gaps
modules.description: Learners study the categories of AI diagnostic error—false negatives, distribution shift, and adversarial vulnerabilities—and trace their real-world consequences for patients. They work through case analyses to determine how clinicians, hospitals, and developers share responsibility when an AI-assisted diagnosis causes harm. By the end, learners can describe the oversight structures and fail-safes that reduce the likelihood and impact of AI errors in clinical settings.

modules.title: The Future Doctor-AI Partnership
modules.sub: Envisioning collaboration between clinicians and intelligent systems
modules.description: Learners synthesize insights from the full course to construct a vision of how physicians, nurses, and AI systems will share decision-making authority in near-future healthcare environments. They debate questions of trust, professional identity, and regulatory readiness that will shape how quickly and safely that partnership evolves. The module closes with learners drafting a personal or organizational position on how to engage with AI tools responsibly in their own health context.

synopsis: Explore how artificial intelligence is transforming hospitals, clinics, and research labs — from diagnosing cancer to accelerating drug development. This course helps patients, caregivers, and health professionals understand both the promise and the peril of AI in medicine.
rationale: A demand score of 7/10 combined with a corpus similarity of only 0.65 against the tangentially related 'हर रोज़ का AI' confirms that healthcare AI deserves a dedicated, substantive course distinct from everyday AI overviews.
